content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

For many years, web developers had a small problem that never felt small in real projects.
We wanted to search, filter, and fetch data without changing anything on the server.
For simple cases, GET was perfect.
GET /products?brand=Samsung&minPrice=10000&maxPrice=30000But then applications became more complex.
Search filters became bigger.
Users wanted sorting, pagination, date ranges, multiple brands, multiple categories, ratings, availability, location, and more.
Suddenly the URL started looking like this:
GET /products?brand=Samsung&brand=OnePlus&category=mobile&minPrice=10000&maxPrice=30000&rating=4&inStock=true&sort=price_asc&page=1&size=20And in real applications, it can get much worse.
So developers often moved the search body into a POST request.
POST /products/search
Content-Type: application/json{
"brands": ["Samsung", "OnePlus"],
"category": "mobile",
"minPrice": 10000,
"maxPrice": 30000,
"minRating": 4,
"inStock": true,
"sort": "price_asc"
}This worked, but it was not perfect.
Why?
Because POST usually means “create something” or “perform an action that may change server state.”
But a search request does not create anything.
It only asks for data. That is exactly where the new HTTP QUERY method becomes useful.
In June 2026, the HTTP QUERY method became RFC 10008, a Proposed Standard. It is designed for requests that need a body, like POST, but are still safe and idempotent, like GET.
What is QUERY?
QUERY is a new HTTP method for read-only requests that need a request body.
Think of it like this:
QUERY /products/search
Content-Type: application/json{
"brands": ["Samsung", "OnePlus"],
"category": "mobile",
"minPrice": 10000,
"maxPrice": 30000,
"minRating": 4,
"inStock": true,
"sort": "price_asc"
}This request says:
“I am sending some search instructions in the body. Please process them and return the result. But do not change anything.”
The official RFC says that QUERY asks the target resource to process the enclosed content in a safe and idempotent way, then return the result.
That one sentence is the whole point.
Safe and idempotent, in simple words
Two words are important here: safe and idempotent.
Safe
Safe means the client is not asking the server to change data.
For example:
QUERY /products/searchThis should not create a product.
It should not update a product.
It should not delete a product.
It should only return matching products.
Idempotent
Idempotent means sending the same request again should have the same effect.
For example, if the client sends the same QUERY request five times, it should not create five records or trigger five payments.
It should simply return the result five times.
This is one reason QUERY is more meaningful than using POST for read-only searches.
GET vs POST vs QUERYHere is the simple difference:
| Method | Has body? | Changes data? | Best use |
| ------ | ---------- | ------------- | ------------------------------ |
| GET | Usually no | No | Simple read requests |
| POST | Yes | Often yes | Create data or trigger actions |
| QUERY | Yes | No | Complex read-only searches |
So, QUERY does not replace GET.
For simple requests, GET is still great.
GET /products/101It also does not replace POST.
For creating data, POST is still correct.
POST /productsBut for complex searches, QUERY finally gives us a cleaner option.
Why developers should care
Let’s say you are building an e-commerce application.
Your product search screen has many filters:

• keyword

• category

• brand list

• minimum price

• maximum price

• minimum rating

• stock availability

• sort order

• page number

• page size
Using GET, the URL becomes long and difficult to read.
Using POST, the API works, but the method does not clearly express that this is a read-only operation.
Using QUERY, the API becomes clear:
QUERY /api/products/search
Content-Type: application/jsonThe method itself tells the story.
This is a query. It has a body. It should not modify data.
Spring Boot example
Now let’s implement a simple Spring Boot example.
Important note: as of the current Spring Framework documentation, RequestMethod lists common methods like GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS, and TRACE, but not QUERY.
So for now, we should not write something like this:
@RequestMapping(method = RequestMethod.QUERY) // This does not exist todayInstead, we can map the endpoint and check the HTTP method manually.
Request DTO
package com.example.demo.dto;import java.math.BigDecimal;
import java.util.List;public record ProductSearchRequest(
String keyword,
String category,
List<String> brands,
BigDecimal minPrice,
BigDecimal maxPrice,
Integer minRating,
Boolean inStock,
String sort,
Integer page,
Integer size
) {
}Response DTO
package com.example.demo.dto;import java.math.BigDecimal;public record ProductResponse(
Long id,
String name,
String brand,
BigDecimal price,
double rating,
boolean inStock
) {
}Service class
This is a simple demo service. In a real project, you may use JPA Specification, QueryDSL, Criteria API, Elasticsearch, or any search engine.
package com.example.demo.service;import com.example.demo.dto.ProductResponse;
import com.example.demo.dto.ProductSearchRequest;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;import java.math.BigDecimal;
import java.util.List;@Service
public class ProductService { public List<ProductResponse> search(ProductSearchRequest request) { // Demo response only.
// In real life, apply filters from request and query the database. return List.of(
new ProductResponse(
1L,
"Samsung Galaxy A55",
"Samsung",
new BigDecimal("32999"),
4.4,
true
),
new ProductResponse(
2L,
"OnePlus Nord 4",
"OnePlus",
new BigDecimal("29999"),
4.3,
true
)
);
}
}Controller class
package com.example.demo.controller;import com.example.demo.dto.ProductResponse;
import com.example.demo.dto.ProductSearchRequest;
import com.example.demo.service.ProductService;
import jakarta.servlet.http.HttpServletRequest;
import org.springframework.http.HttpHeaders;
import org.springframework.http.HttpStatus;
import org.springframework.http.MediaType;
import org.springframework.http.ResponseEntity;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RequestBody;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RequestMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;import java.util.List;
import java.util.Map;@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/products")
public class ProductSearchController { private static final String QUERY_METHOD = "QUERY"; private final ProductService productService; public ProductSearchController(ProductService productService) {
this.productService = productService;
} @RequestMapping(
value = "/search",
consumes = MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON_VALUE,
produces = MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON_VALUE
)
public ResponseEntity<?> searchProducts(
HttpServletRequest httpRequest,
@RequestBody ProductSearchRequest searchRequest
) { if (!QUERY_METHOD.equalsIgnoreCase(httpRequest.getMethod())) {
return ResponseEntity
.status(HttpStatus.METHOD_NOT_ALLOWED)
.header(HttpHeaders.ALLOW, QUERY_METHOD)
.header("Accept-Query", MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON_VALUE)
.body(Map.of(
"error", "Use QUERY for this read-only search endpoint"
));
} List<ProductResponse> products = productService.search(searchRequest); return ResponseEntity
.ok()
.header("Accept-Query", MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON_VALUE)
.header(HttpHeaders.CACHE_CONTROL, "max-age=60")
.body(products);
}
}The Accept-Query response header can be used by a resource to show that it supports the QUERY method and to identify supported query media types.
Test with curl
curl -X QUERY "http://localhost:8080/api/products/search" \
-H "Content-Type: application/json" \
-d '{
"keyword": "phone",
"category": "mobile",
"brands": ["Samsung", "OnePlus"],
"minPrice": 10000,
"maxPrice": 35000,
"minRating": 4,
"inStock": true,
"sort": "price_asc",
"page": 0,
"size": 10
}'Expected response:
[
{
"id": 1,
"name": "Samsung Galaxy A55",
"brand": "Samsung",
"price": 32999,
"rating": 4.4,
"inStock": true
},
{
"id": 2,
"name": "OnePlus Nord 4",
"brand": "OnePlus",
"price": 29999,
"rating": 4.3,
"inStock": true
}
]Calling QUERY from Java
Java’s built-in HttpClient can send a custom method name using .method().
package com.example.demo.client;import java.net.URI;
import java.net.http.HttpClient;
import java.net.http.HttpRequest;
import java.net.http.HttpResponse;public class QueryClientExample { public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception { String json = """
{
"keyword": "phone",
"category": "mobile",
"brands": ["Samsung", "OnePlus"],
"minPrice": 10000,
"maxPrice": 35000,
"minRating": 4,
"inStock": true,
"sort": "price_asc",
"page": 0,
"size": 10
}
"""; HttpRequest request = HttpRequest.newBuilder()
.uri(URI.create("http://localhost:8080/api/products/search"))
.header("Content-Type", "application/json")
.method("QUERY", HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.ofString(json))
.build(); HttpClient client = HttpClient.newHttpClient(); HttpResponse<String> response = client.send(
request,
HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString()
); System.out.println(response.statusCode());
System.out.println(response.body());
}
}What about CORS?
If your frontend runs in a browser, QUERY will require a CORS preflight request because it is not a CORS-safelisted method. RFC 10008 also notes this behavior.
In Spring Boot, you can allow it like this:
package com.example.demo.config;import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;
import org.springframework.web.servlet.config.annotation.CorsRegistry;
import org.springframework.web.servlet.config.annotation.WebMvcConfigurer;@Configuration
public class WebConfig implements WebMvcConfigurer { @Override
public void addCorsMappings(CorsRegistry registry) {
registry.addMapping("/api/**")
.allowedOrigins("https://your-frontend-domain.com")
.allowedMethods("GET", "POST", "QUERY", "OPTIONS")
.allowedHeaders("Content-Type", "Authorization")
.maxAge(3600);
}
}Should you use QUERY today?
The honest answer is: carefully.
QUERY is now standardized, but ecosystem support is still growing.
Before using it in production, check:

• Does your API gateway allow QUERY?

• Does your load balancer pass it correctly?

• Does your CDN understand it?

• Does your client library support custom HTTP methods?

• Does your observability tool log it correctly?

• Does your security layer allow it?

• Does your frontend need CORS configuration?
For internal APIs, service-to-service APIs, and controlled environments, QUERY can be a great fit.
For public APIs, it may be smart to support both options for some time:
QUERY /api/products/searchand
POST /api/products/searchThe POST version can act as a fallback while clients and tools catch up.
When not to use QUERY
Do not use QUERY for actions that change data.
Bad example:
QUERY /orders/createThis is wrong because creating an order changes server state.
Use POST instead.
POST /ordersAlso, do not use QUERY just because it is new.
For simple reads, keep using GET.
GET /products/101The best use case for QUERY is a complex read-only request where the input is too large or too structured for a clean URL.
Final thoughts
For years, developers had to choose between two imperfect options.
GET was semantically correct for reading data, but not comfortable for complex request bodies.
POST allowed request bodies, but did not clearly say, “This is only a query.”
QUERY fills that gap.
It gives us a method that says exactly what we mean:
“I am sending query input in the body. Please return the result. Do not change anything.”
That sounds small, but for API design, it is a big improvement.
In 2026, QUERY may not replace your existing APIs overnight. But it gives backend developers, API designers, and platform teams a cleaner language for complex read-only operations.
And sometimes, better language is exactly what good API design needs.
